we have reviewed the proposed regulations governing gifts
under Government Code secEion 8950{ et seq. on the whole they
appear quite clear and cover the subject area. Hor'/ever, there are

u-:e::native, we need informal advice in the foiiowing areas.
First, there is a probLem with reportj-ng LlckeEs or passes
that have been given to the Governor himself, ncl h-is office'

allowing disqualifications, 
che regulations seem Iinited

occasiois when the gifr could potenCially influence a governmental
decis j"on. In conErast, the Governor is given many complimentary
tickets or Passes tha! have no connection with any Particular
governmenEaL decision.
In fact, these are courtesy tickets or Passes given to the
Governor simply because he is the Governor, by the entity
operating the event or business. These t.ickets or passes are
glneraII! nontransferable and regarded by the companies as part of
€tei. p,rlrtic relations budget. If the Governor did use the
t.icket;, it would be a media coup. In lj'ght of this, there is
some feeling that returning the tickets woul'd be an insult' 
In
most cases, these tickets or Passes do not make it to the
Governor's attention and are never used. Further, given the
volume of mail and the fact that alt mail i.s read and then routed
to an appropriate person for response, by the Eime -Lhe tickets or
passes ire trought to the attention of Scheduling or Legal
iff.irs, 
the 30 day time limit. for return has expired '
The Governor is in a unique Position and Ehe regulations do
not clearly address this issue. For the record, any tickets or
pu"="" that are used are reported. The question. remains: can all
Lf the." tickets and passes be disqualified if they are not used?

The second question is: 
how does this office go about
assigning a "fair narket value" to gifts that defy evaluating?
For example, the German Ambassador presented Governor wilson with
a piece of the Berlin WaII. This is a gift of history for which
it is difficult, 
if not impossibLe, to ascertain a monetary va1ue,
There are other gifts of this kind, and, I am sure, these vrill not
be the Last. The Governor of Cafifornia is probably more likely
to receive gifts of this nature than other State officials.
Additionally, I wou).d ]ike to confirm your oral advice
concerning the regular use of typical California products. You
agreed that a pub).ic official 
could solicit fruit, 
produce, or
other California grown or produced products to be served as
refreshments or given as gifts to visiting foreign dignitaries to
promote California business and trade. These products are not for
personal use or gain, but to showcase California products and
induce foreign interests to trade or otherwise do business with
California.
Lj.ke some of the gifts mentioned above, this is a unique
situation in which public officials 
would have an opportunity to
pronote business and increase revenues for California. 
You agreed
these items would not need to be reported as gifts.
As you can see, regulations which address the unj-que issues,
as weII as the routine would be very heJ.pful . Even though this
office probably encounters the unique more often/ I am sure other
of f j.cials must be faced with the same dilemmas. For this reason,
we are suggesting tshat regulations might be f ormul,ated to address
these concerns.
If you do not feel that regulations are appropriate, this
office would appreciate an informal opinion to guide our staff,
Thank you for taking the time to consider our concerns, If you
have any questions, feel free to contact me,
As a footnote, there is one other portion of the regulations,
2 CCR S 18945 et seq. that we wiII be addressing in a separate
Ietter. 
These provisions affect a larger number of State
officials.
S incere Iy,
Janice Rogers Bror,rn
Legal- Affairs Secretary

sections.question_synthetic: The Governor's office seeks informal FPPC advice on three gift-related issues: (1) whether complimentary tickets/passes given to the Governor that are not used can be disqualified from reporting requirements; (2) how to assign fair market value to unique or historically significant gifts with no clear monetary valuation; and (3) confirmation that California products solicited and given to foreign dignitaries to promote California trade do not require reporting as gifts.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from the Governor's office to FPPC, not an FPPC response. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections present. Document raises three distinct policy questions regarding gift regulations under Government Code section 89501 et seq. Sender requests either regulatory amendments or informal advice letter.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: The Governor's office seeks informal FPPC advice on three gift-related issues: (1) whether complimentary tickets/passes given to the Governor that are not used can be disqualified from reporting requirements; (2) how to assign fair market value to unique or historically significant gifts with no clear monetary valuation; and (3) confirmation that California products solicited and given to foreign dignitaries to promote California trade do not require reporting as gifts.

embedding.summary: The Governor's Legal Affairs office requests informal FPPC advice on gift reporting and disqualification issues specific to the Governor's office, including problems with unused complimentary tickets, valuation of unique historical gifts, and the treatment of California products given to visiting dignitaries for trade promotion purposes.
